events.event_label: Thylakoid membrane contains galactolipids, MGDG, DGDG, sulfolipids, SQDG, phospholipids and PG
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s006: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees. PMC9183074.p026.s008: The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.

events.event_label: High Temperature exposure alters total fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p013.s001: 2.3. PMC9183074.p013.s002: Alteration of Leaf Total Fatty Acids after High Temperature Exposure

events.event_label: HT reduces PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction. PMC9183074.p024.s005: The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT. PMC9183074.p024.s006: In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p024.s007: Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.

events.event_label: HT reduces membrane thermostability and photosynthetic efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged. PMC9183074.p025.s008: This suggested that HT caused a reduction in membrane thermostability in IR64 and KDML105 and resulted in markedly decreased photosynthetic efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm) (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p025.s009: Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded. PMC9183074.p025.s010: Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules.

events.event_label: HT stress decreases electrolyte leakage and increases Fv/Fm
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s006: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars. PMC9183074.p027.s007: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s008: Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage. PMC9183074.p027.s009: Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).

events.event_label: Capric acid and other saturated fatty acids detected in rice
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s006: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars. PMC9183074.p027.s007: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s008: Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage. PMC9183074.p027.s009: Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).

events.event_label: HT reduces unsaturated fatty acids and increases membrane stability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s001: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p043.s002: In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1). PMC9183074.p043.s003: The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability. PMC9183074.p043.s004: Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased.

events.event_label: HT increases electrolyte leakage and lipid peroxidation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p126.s001: Effect of HT on electrolyte leakage (A) and lipid peroxidation (B) in four rice cultivars (N22, CN1, KDML105 and IR64). PMC9183074.p126.s002: Each bar represents mean ± SE (n = 3–4). PMC9183074.p126.s003: Asterisk (*) indicates significant difference between control and HT in each rice cultivar at p ≤ 0.05 by t test. PMC9183074.p126.s004: Different capital letters indicate significant difference of control group in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT, and small letters indicate significant difference of HT in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT.

events.event_label: HT decreases total fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p014.s001: Total fatty acids in seedlings of all four rice cultivars after an exposure to HT sharply reduced by 1.4–2.0-fold compared to the controls (Figure 3). PMC9183074.p014.s002: Significant differences in total fatty acids were noted among genotypes. PMC9183074.p014.s003: The highest total fatty acid content was found in CN1 for both the non-stress (10.769 µg µL−1) and HT-stressed (7.608 µg µL−1) plants. PMC9183074.p014.s004: The HT-stressed plants of N22, KDML105 and IR 64 had similar contents of total fatty acids, varying from 3.89 to 7.61 µg µL−1.

events.event_label: HT increases palmitoleic acid abundance in rice
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s003: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p019.s005: In the non-stress plants, palmitoleic acid (C16:1) was present in N22 and IR64 but absent from CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p019.s006: Upon HT stress, the content of this acid significantly increased in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p019.s007: The content of palmitoleic acid in the HT plants of CN1 and KDML105 was present several folds higher than those in N22 and IR64.

events.event_label: Heat stress affects lipid composition in thylakoid membranes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s001: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress. PMC9183074.p027.s002: Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses. PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).

events.event_label: ROS affects cellular membrane, oxygen evolving complex and PSII
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

events.event_label: HT impairs PSII function leading to inhibition of electron transport, reduced carbon fixation, chlorophyll degradation and leaf senescence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: HT damages cellular membranes causing lipid peroxidation, leaf senescence and affecting unsaturated fatty acids via ROS
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p005.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT. PMC9183074.p005.s002: HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence. PMC9183074.p005.s003: The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.

events.event_label: HT stress reduces photosynthetic efficiency via ROS-mediated damage

events.event_label: saturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s001: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1. PMC9183074.p017.s002: Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

events.event_label: High Temperature stress damages cellular membranes, decreasing stability and increasing electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: HT induces membrane fluidity increase, lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: Leaf fatty acid associates with HT tolerance and sensitivity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s001: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. PMC9183074.p002.s002: This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes. PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).

events.event_label: High Temperature disrupts cell membranes and induces leaf senescence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p005.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT. PMC9183074.p005.s002: HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence. PMC9183074.p005.s003: The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.

events.event_label: High Temperature changes leaf fatty acid composition and physiological parameters
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s021: Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids. PMC9183074.p007.s022: Hu et al. proposed that an increase in unsaturated fatty acids enhanced lipid thermostability in a photosynthetic membrane and might contribute to heat tolerance in various species. PMC9183074.p007.s023: Therefore, the experimental results inferring the relationship between lipid composition (degree of fatty acid saturation/unsaturation) and heat tolerance in various species are still inconclusive. PMC9183074.p007.s024: Thus, this research studied the HT responses of two Thai rice cultivars compared to a standard heat tolerance and a heat-sensitive rice genotype, in terms of physiological parameters and leaf fatty acid composition changes.

events.event_label: unsaturated fatty acids affect membrane fluidity and improve heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s012: Moreover, soybean and peanut also responded to HT by decreasing lipid unsaturation levels to maintain membrane fluidity, and the magnitudes of reduction were greater for heat-tolerant than for heat-sensitive genotypes. PMC9183074.p006.s013: In contrast, some experiments demonstrated that higher degrees of unsaturated fatty acids can maintain membrane fluidity under HT. PMC9183074.p006.s014: For example, tall fescue genotypes that exhibited an increased degree of unsaturated fatty acids showed an improvement in heat tolerance.

events.event_label: HT alters fatty acid composition, decreasing saturated and unsaturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p015.s003: Only N22 and KDML105 showed slightly significant reductions in total saturated fatty acid contents after exposure to HT. PMC9183074.p015.s004: The highest and the lowest total saturated fatty acid contents after HT treatment were found in CN1 (1.606 µg µL−1) and KDML105 (0.995 µg µL−1), respectively. PMC9183074.p015.s005: In contrast, unsaturated fatty acid contents in all rice cultivars were markedly reduced compared to the controls. PMC9183074.p015.s006: KDML105 showed the sharpest decline in total unsaturated fatty acids (2.6-fold reduction from the control), and the amount under HT was lowest in this cultivar. PMC9183074.p015.s007: Conversely, the lowest reduction in total unsaturated fatty acid under HT was found in CN1.

events.event_label: High Temperature alters lipid membrane structure and fluidity linked to composition and saturation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s015: Previous reports in rice showed that HT led to significantly increased lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage, in cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s016: Dular, IR64 and KDML105 at seedling, and in cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s017: IR64 and KDML105 at the dough grain stage. PMC9183074.p007.s018: HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level. PMC9183074.p007.s019: Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C).

events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. events.event_label: Lipid composition and leaf fatty acids associate with heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s006: Better maintenance of membrane thermostability and thicker cuticles in heat-tolerant genotypes lead to less electrolyte leakage, lower lipid peroxidation and stable Fv/Fm compared to the more sensitive genotypes. PMC9183074.p043.s007: Thus, this study suggested that there may be some relationships between the lipid composition of the thylakoid membrane and cuticle characteristics in a rice leaf, and their ability to tolerate HT. PMC9183074.p043.s008: From this study, changes in leaf fatty acid compositions can potentially be used as indicators for heat tolerance in rice.

events.event_label: HT reduces photosynthetic efficiency and increases ROS

PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

events.event_label: heat stress alters lipid composition
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s001: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress. PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).

events.event_label: HT induces lipid peroxidation leading to electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

events.event_label: HT damages membranes and increases electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: HT decreases PSII efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction. PMC9183074.p024.s005: The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT. PMC9183074.p024.s006: In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p024.s007: Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.

events.event_label: HT decreases unsaturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s001: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p043.s002: In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1). PMC9183074.p043.s003: The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability. PMC9183074.p043.s004: Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased.

events.event_label: Saturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s001: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1. PMC9183074.p017.s002: Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

events.event_label: MGDG, SQDG, and PG detected in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s006: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees. PMC9183074.p026.s008: The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.

events.event_label: High Temperature occurs_in Rice Seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p001.s001: High Temperature Alters Leaf Lipid Membrane Composition Associated with Photochemistry of PSII and Membrane Thermostability in Rice Seedlings

events.event_label: HT affects Rice
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s001: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. PMC9183074.p002.s002: This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes. PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).

events.event_label: HT increases_abundance_of membrane fluidity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s001: The fluidity of lipid membrane is important for membrane function that depends on the interaction between lipid and protein compositions. PMC9183074.p006.s002: The increase in membrane fluidity resulted from an HT-induced higher degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p006.s003: Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene. PMC9183074.p006.s004: Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT.

events.event_label: FAD increases_abundance_of membrane fluidity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s001: The fluidity of lipid membrane is important for membrane function that depends on the interaction between lipid and protein compositions. PMC9183074.p006.s002: The increase in membrane fluidity resulted from an HT-induced higher degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p006.s003: Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene. PMC9183074.p006.s004: Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT.

events.event_label: HT stress decreases_abundance_of Fv/Fm
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p010.s001: Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.822 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p010.s002: However, when subjected to HT stress, Fv/Fm significantly decreased in IR64 (the heat sensitive check), KDML105 and CN1. PMC9183074.p010.s003: In contrast, the heat-tolerant genotype (N22) maintained a high Fv/Fm value (approximately 0.806). PMC9183074.p010.s004: Heat stressed CN1 still had relatively high Fv/Fm that was slightly higher than N22.

PMC9183074.p024.s007: Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex. relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival.

relations.evidence_preview: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s001: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. PMC9183074.p002.s002: This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes. PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes.
Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days.

Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).
Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm.

This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings.

relations.evidence_preview: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s001: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2. PMC9183074.p019.s002: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions. PMC9183074.p019.s003: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2.

Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions.
Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings.
In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings.
In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
In the non-stress plants, palmitoleic acid (C16:1) was present in N22 and IR64 but absent from CN1 and KDML105.
Upon HT stress, the content of this acid significantly increased in all cultivars.
The content of palmitoleic acid in the HT plants of CN1 and KDML105 was present several folds higher than those in N22 and IR64.

The content of palmitoleic acid in the HT plants of CN1 and KDML105 was present several folds higher than those in N22 and IR64.

evidence_context_sentences.text: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield.

HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis.
Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.
The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.
The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT.

In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1).

Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.
Similarly, Dongsansuk et al. reported that Fv/Fm significantly reduced in the heat-sensitive rice PT60 but was stable in Dular after exposure to 45 °C for 30 min.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.

relations.evidence_preview: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

evidence_context_sentences.text: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.

HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death.
MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death.
MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged. This suggested that HT caused a reduction in membrane thermostability in IR64 and KDML105 and resulted in markedly decreased photosynthetic efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm) (Figure 1).

Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded. Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules.
Consequently, the light reaction taking place in the thylakoid membrane was inhibited, resulting in a reduction in the efficiency of the PSII photochemistry as seen in IR64 and KDML105 (Figure 1).
Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded.
Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules.

PMC9183074.p026.s004: An insight into the relationship between the observed physiological parameters under HT and alterations in leaf fatty acid content and composition was manifested in this study. PMC9183074.p026.s006: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s007: This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Diverse plant lipids consist of polyunsaturated fatty acids, mainly linoleic acid (C18:2) and linolenic acid (C18:3), monounsaturated fatty acids, mainly oleic acid (C18:1) and saturated fatty acids, mainly palmitic acid (C16:0) and stearic acid (C18:0).
An insight into the relationship between the observed physiological parameters under HT and alterations in leaf fatty acid content and composition was manifested in this study.
Under HT, total fatty acid content decreased in all rice cultivars (Figure 3, Table 1).

A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1).
This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1).

The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG).
Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.
The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1).
This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees.

The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG).
Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.
The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability.

relations.subject: heat shock

evidence.text: In contrast to this study, Wang et al. reported that for temperate species such as Arabidopsis, heat shock at 38 °C for 6 h did not induce any changes in unsaturated fatty acids but prolonged treatment at warm temperature (28 °C, 7 d), resulting in an enrichment of these acids to achieve an optimum level of membrane fluidity.

relations.subject: heat stress

evidence.text: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress.

relations.evidence_preview: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s001: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress. PMC9183074.p027.s002: Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses. PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress.

Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses.

Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105.
In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).

relations.subject: HT stress

evidence.text: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105.

relations.evidence_preview: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s006: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars. PMC9183074.p027.s007: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s008: Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage. PMC9183074.p027.s009: Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).

evidence_context_sentences.text: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars.

Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105.

Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage.
Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).

PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).

evidence_context_sentences.text: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars.

Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105.

Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage.
Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).

In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).
Capric acid (C10:0) was not found in N22 and IR64.
Capric acid (C10:0) occurs in coconut oil, and it is used as an aromatic chemical in the manufacture of perfumes.
This suggested that Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105, especially KDML105 or Thai Jasmine rice, is an aromatic rice and has volatile aromatic compounds that make the aroma and flavour in the rice.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance.

relations.evidence_preview: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s001: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p043.s002: In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1). PMC9183074.p043.s003: The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability. PMC9183074.p043.s004: Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased.

evidence_context_sentences.text: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance.

In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1).
The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability.

Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased. These long-chain fatty acids may contribute to increased thickness of the cuticle, which helps prevent excessive water loss and reflects strong light, resulting in lower HT-induced damage.

evidence.text: Thus, this study suggested that there may be some relationships between the lipid composition of the thylakoid membrane and cuticle characteristics in a rice leaf, and their ability to tolerate HT.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s006: Better maintenance of membrane thermostability and thicker cuticles in heat-tolerant genotypes lead to less electrolyte leakage, lower lipid peroxidation and stable Fv/Fm compared to the more sensitive genotypes. entities.selected_description: Any aliphatic monocarboxylic acid derived from or contained in esterified form in an animal or vegetable fat, oil or wax. Natural fatty acids commonly have a chain of 4 to 28 carbons (usually unbranched and even-numbered), which may be saturated or unsaturated. By extension, the term is sometimes used to embrace all acyclic aliphatic carboxylic acids.

entities.selected_description: The fixation of carbon dioxide (CO2) as glucose in the chloroplasts of C3 plants; uses ATP and NADPH formed in the light reactions of photosynthesis; carbon dioxide reacts with ribulose 1,5-bisphosphate (catalyzed by the function of ribulose-bisphosphate carboxylase) to yield two molecules of 3-phosphoglycerate; these are then phosphorylated by ATP to 1,3-bisphosphateglyceraldehyde which, in turn, is then reduced by NADPH to glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate. The glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate is converted to fructose 5-phosphate and ribulose 5-phosphate by aldolase and other enzymes; the ribulose 5-phosphate is phosphorylated by ATP to ribulose 1,5-bisphosphate.

selected_concepts.label: reductive pentose-phosphate cycle

The glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate is converted to fructose 5-phosphate and ribulose 5-phosphate by aldolase and other enzymes; the ribulose 5-phosphate is phosphorylated by ATP to ribulose 1,5-bisphosphate.

entities.selected_description: A photosystem that contains a pheophytin-quinone reaction center with associated accessory pigments and electron carriers. In cyanobacteria and chloroplasts, in the presence of light, PSII functions as a water-plastoquinone oxidoreductase, transferring electrons from water to plastoquinone, whereas other photosynthetic bacteria carry out anoxygenic photosynthesis and oxidize other compounds to re-reduce the photoreaction center.

entities.selected_description: Any aliphatic monocarboxylic acid derived from or contained in esterified form in an animal or vegetable fat, oil or wax. Natural fatty acids commonly have a chain of 4 to 28 carbons (usually unbranched and even-numbered), which may be saturated or unsaturated. By extension, the term is sometimes used to embrace all acyclic aliphatic carboxylic acids.

sentences.text: High Temperature Alters Leaf Lipid Membrane Composition Associated with Photochemistry of PSII and Membrane Thermostability in Rice Seedlings

Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes.
Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days.

Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).
Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm.
HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function.

Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB.
The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function.
This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.
The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT.

HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence.
The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.
The fluidity of lipid membrane is important for membrane function that depends on the interaction between lipid and protein compositions.
The increase in membrane fluidity resulted from an HT-induced higher degree of lipid unsaturation.
Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene.
Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT.

Moreover, soybean and peanut also responded to HT by decreasing lipid unsaturation levels to maintain membrane fluidity, and the magnitudes of reduction were greater for heat-tolerant than for heat-sensitive genotypes.
In contrast, some experiments demonstrated that higher degrees of unsaturated fatty acids can maintain membrane fluidity under HT.
For example, tall fescue genotypes that exhibited an increased degree of unsaturated fatty acids showed an improvement in heat tolerance.
The decline in photosynthetic pigments as affected by HT was found in tall fescue grass, in seedlings of rice cv.
Riceberry, and in rice cvs. IR64 and KDML105 at the dough grain stage.
Moreover, HT also stimulated lipid peroxidation, resulting in a decline in cellular membrane thermal tolerance.

Previous reports in rice showed that HT led to significantly increased lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage, in cvs. Dular, IR64 and KDML105 at seedling, and in cvs. IR64 and KDML105 at the dough grain stage.
HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level.
Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C).
In tall fescue turfgrass, increased lipid unsaturation was reported when exposed to HT (40/35 °C, 14/10 h), but in wheat under HT, there was a decline in the degree of lipid unsaturation.
Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids.
Therefore, the experimental results inferring the relationship between lipid composition (degree of fatty acid saturation/unsaturation) and heat tolerance in various species are still inconclusive.

Thus, this research studied the HT responses of two Thai rice cultivars compared to a standard heat tolerance and a heat-sensitive rice genotype, in terms of physiological parameters and leaf fatty acid composition changes.
Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.
80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.
822 (Figure 1).
However, when subjected to HT stress, Fv/Fm significantly decreased in IR64 (the heat sensitive check), KDML105 and CN1.
All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.
16% (Figure 2A).
When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL.
Total fatty acids in seedlings of all four rice cultivars after an exposure to HT sharply reduced by 1.
0-fold compared to the controls (Figure 3).
Significant differences in total fatty acids were noted among genotypes.
Total fatty acid was typically composed of total saturated and total unsaturated fatty acid as shown in Figure 4.
Total saturated fatty acid contents in seedlings of all rice cultivars were significantly lower than total unsaturated fatty acids.
Only N22 and KDML105 showed slightly significant reductions in total saturated fatty acid contents after exposure to HT.
The highest and the lowest total saturated fatty acid contents after HT treatment were found in CN1 (1.
606 µg µL−1) and KDML105 (0.
995 µg µL−1), respectively.
In contrast, unsaturated fatty acid contents in all rice cultivars were markedly reduced compared to the controls.
A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1.

Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions.
Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.
A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2.

Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions.
Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings.
In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
In the non-stress plants, palmitoleic acid (C16:1) was present in N22 and IR64 but absent from CN1 and KDML105.
Upon HT stress, the content of this acid significantly increased in all cultivars.
The content of palmitoleic acid in the HT plants of CN1 and KDML105 was present several folds higher than those in N22 and IR64.
Changes in mono- and polyunsaturated fatty acids are shown in Figure 5.
Monounsaturated fatty acids in all rice cultivars were several folds lower than polyunsaturated fatty acids.
Upon HT exposure, monounsaturated fatty acids significantly increased in N22 and IR64, but significantly decreased in Thai rice, CN1.
Polyunsaturated fatty acids in seedlings of all rice cultivars markedly decreased under HT.
The highest Sat:Unsat under both control and HT condition were found in KDML105 (Table 3).
Sharp increases in Sat:Unsat after exposure to HT occurred in all rice cultivars in the range between 1.
High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield.

HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis.
Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.
The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT.

In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1).

Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.
Similarly, Dongsansuk et al. reported that Fv/Fm significantly reduced in the heat-sensitive rice PT60 but was stable in Dular after exposure to 45 °C for 30 min.
Dular is known to be a heat-tolerant rice variety comparable to N22.
However, Sailaja et al. stated that in response to heat stress, Fv/Fm in N22 was stable at the reproductive stage but reduced at the vegetative stage.
This indicated that the ability to maintain PSII efficiency varies with developmental stage.
CN1 showed the highest Fv/Fm level, although its Fv/Fm declined after HT exposure (Figure 1).
The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.
The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive.

HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death.
MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged. This suggested that HT caused a reduction in membrane thermostability in IR64 and KDML105 and resulted in markedly decreased photosynthetic efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm) (Figure 1).

Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded. Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules.
Consequently, the light reaction taking place in the thylakoid membrane was inhibited, resulting in a reduction in the efficiency of the PSII photochemistry as seen in IR64 and KDML105 (Figure 1).

Diverse plant lipids consist of polyunsaturated fatty acids, mainly linoleic acid (C18:2) and linolenic acid (C18:3), monounsaturated fatty acids, mainly oleic acid (C18:1) and saturated fatty acids, mainly palmitic acid (C16:0) and stearic acid (C18:0).
An insight into the relationship between the observed physiological parameters under HT and alterations in leaf fatty acid content and composition was manifested in this study.
Under HT, total fatty acid content decreased in all rice cultivars (Figure 3, Table 1).

A comparison between the two Thai rice cultivars revealed that under HT, CN1 showed the lowest reduction compared to the control and contained the highest total fatty acid content, but KDML105 showed the highest reduction and the lowest amount of total fatty acids (Figure 3, Table 1).
This suggested that HT significantly changed total leaf fatty acids, and different genotypes were affected to different degrees.

The thylakoid membrane is reported to be the most sensitive site severely damaged by heat stress and the degree of damage is related to plant survival. The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG).
Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.
The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability.

In contrast to this study, Wang et al. reported that for temperate species such as Arabidopsis, heat shock at 38 °C for 6 h did not induce any changes in unsaturated fatty acids but prolonged treatment at warm temperature (28 °C, 7 d), resulting in an enrichment of these acids to achieve an optimum level of membrane fluidity.
Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress.

Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses.

Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105.
In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).
A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars.

Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105.

Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage.
Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).
Capric acid (C10:0) was not found in N22 and IR64.
Capric acid (C10:0) occurs in coconut oil, and it is used as an aromatic chemical in the manufacture of perfumes.
This suggested that Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105, especially KDML105 or Thai Jasmine rice, is an aromatic rice and has volatile aromatic compounds that make the aroma and flavour in the rice.
HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance.

In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1).
The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability.

Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased. These long-chain fatty acids may contribute to increased thickness of the cuticle, which helps prevent excessive water loss and reflects strong light, resulting in lower HT-induced damage.
Thus, this study suggested that there may be some relationships between the lipid composition of the thylakoid membrane and cuticle characteristics in a rice leaf, and their ability to tolerate HT.
From this study, changes in leaf fatty acid compositions can potentially be used as indicators for heat tolerance in rice.
